nameMeanings.name: Bartholomew
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: from the Middle English personal name Bertilmew, Bertelmy, a borrowing of the Old French form of the Biblical personal name Bartholomew (from Latin Bartholomaeus; Hebrew ‘son of Talmai’, said to mean ‘having many furrows’, i.e. rich in land). This was an extremely popular personal name in Christian Europe, with many vernacular derivatives. It derived its popularity from the apostle Saint Bartholomew (Matthew 10:3), who was, among other things, the patron saint of tanners, vintners, and butlers.
As an Irish name, it has been used as an Anglicized form of Mac Pharthaláin (see McFarlane ).

variants.name: McFarlane
meanings: Scottish and northern Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Pharthaláin, a patronymic from the personal name Parthalán, which is most probably from Latin Bartholomaeus (see Bartholomew ). Parthalán, regularly shortened in speech to Parlán, has also been Anglicized as Bartlett . Compare McPartland .
History: McFarlane is a Scottish clan originally associated with Arrochar on Loch Long. — Robert McFarlane came to NY in 1835 and was editor of Scientific American magazine (1848–65).

variants.name: Bara
meanings: Polish: from a shortened vernacular form of the personal name Bartłomiej (see Bartholomew ).

Czech (Bára) and Slovak: from a pet form of the female personal name Barbora (Czech), Barbara (Slovak), or possibly of the male personal name Bartoloměj (Czech), Bartolomej (Slovak); see Bartholomew .
Hungarian: from a short form of the personal name Barabás (see Barabas ).

variants.name: Barak
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Pinhas, Yoram, Avital, Doron, Dov, Erez, Haim, Hanoch, Moshe, Ronit, Shai, Shimon.

Jewish (Israeli): from the Biblical Hebrew personal name Barak, meaning ‘lightning’. The surname mainly represents a Hebraicized form of several original eastern Ashkenazic surnames having similar consonants.

Jewish (eastern Ashkenazic): acronymic surname from the first letters of the Hebrew patronymic phrase Ben Rabi Kalonymos ‘son of rabbi Kalonymos’. The personal name Kalonymos means ‘beautiful name’ in Greek.

variants.name: Bac
meanings: Polish (also Bać): perhaps a variant of Bacz, from a pet form of the personal name Bartłomiej (see Bartholomew ), occuring in southeastern Poland. Aternatively, it may be a metonymic occupational name derived from baca ‘shepherd’. Compare Batz .
French: from bac ‘tub, trough’, probably applied as a metonymic occupational name or as a topographic name.
Slovenian: nickname from bac ‘lambkin’. Compare Batz .

variants.name: Baert
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Aime.
Flemish and Dutch:
from a short form of the personal name Bartholomaeus (see Bartholomew ), or from the female personal name Baerte or Bartha.

variants.name: Baczek
meanings: Polish:
derivative of the personal name Bacz, Bacza, a pet form of Bartłomiej (see Bartholomew ).
(Bączek): from bączek, a diminutive of bąk, which variously means ‘horse-fly’, ‘bittern’, or ‘eagle-owl’ (see Bak ).

variants.name: Arko
meanings: Slovenian: from a pet form of the personal names beginning with Ar-, most likely from Arnej, an old vernacular form of Bartolomej (see Bartholomew ).
Jewish (eastern Ashkenazic): from a pet form of the personal name Aron, Slavic form of the Biblical name Aaron .
West African (Ghana): from an Akan male personal name denoting a first-born boy.

variants.name: Barac
meanings: Croatian: from an old pet form of the personal name Bartolomej (see Bartholomew ) or of its vernacular variants Bartol or Bartul.
Croatian (Barać and Barač): from a derivative of the personal name Bare or Baro, short forms of Bartolomej (see Bartholomew ) or of its vernacular variants Bartol or Bartul.

Serbian (mainly Barać): topographic name derived from bara ‘swamp’ or a habitational name for someone from a place named Bare. It may also be of the same origin as the Croatian names above. Compare Barach .

variants.name: Bach
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Otto, Hans, Kurt, Fritz, Klaus, Horst, Johann, Alfons, Bernhard, Erna, Ernst.

German: topographic name for someone who lived by a stream, Middle High German bach ‘stream’. This surname is also found in some other European countries, most notably in Denmark and France (Alsace and Lorraine), but also in e.g. the Netherlands, Czechia (see also 5 below), and Croatia.
Jewish (Ashkenazic): artificial name from German Bach ‘stream, creek’.

variants.name: Baczewski
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Polish Zygmunt, Bogdan, Bronislaw, Danuta, Jerzy, Zbigniew, Zigmund, Zygfryd.
Polish:
habitational name for someone from a place called Bacze in Masovian Voivodeship.

nameMeanings.name: Mary
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From Latin Maria via Old Welsh Meir. See Mary .

en.lifeSummary: When Mary Bartholomew was born in 1566, in Rattlesden, Suffolk, England, United Kingdom, her father, Thomas Bartholomew, was 24 and her mother, Agnes Abrooke, was 30. She had at least 1 daughter with Roger Rainam. She died in June 1607, in her hometown, at the age of 41.
